title: NGE Anno Us and The Theory of Negative Capability
display_title: NGE Anno Us and The Theory of Negative Capability
wikitext:
[]: NGE, Anno, Us, and The Theory of Negative Capability...             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jun 17, 2010 1:57 am Ok, everyone here knows what a Bardoloter (Shakespeare worshiper) I am, and in rereading J. Dover Wilson's "What Happens in Hamlet" he brings up John Keats' Theory of Negative Capability in regards to one's approach in analyzing Hamlet and Shakespeare in general. Essentially, the theory states that the best artists are in a position of being able to accept uncertainties. He gives as example so many of Hamlet's inconsistencies (Horatio's suspect origins: He wavers between ignorance of Denmark and intimately knowledgeable of it, its people and customs) and seemingly unresolvable mysteries (Hamlet's questionable madness). In fact, he states that this very element of irresolvable uncertainty is what generates the most drama. It's what lead Goethe to claim he could not be a great dramatist because he could not tolerate such loose ends in which he himself didn't know the answers. How this applies to NGE is, I think, obvious. So much has been made of its plotholes, inconsistencies, and gaping gaps in its backstory and mythology that explains all of the SF aspects cogently and satisfiactorily. While many have often scoffed at my attempts to defend these things it occurred to me that the theory of Negative Capability actually backs up a lot of what I've been saying all along, that those uncertainties and lack of knowledge tends to generate a great amount of NGE's drama and tension. To think of it another way, imagine watching ep. 19 (for example) for the first time while knowing precisely what is happening at every moment. Suppose we know that Yui is the Eva's soul and she's coming alive to protect Shinji because of the synch-ratio stuff and that she's eating an S2 to gain POWA and conscience. It seems to me this knowledge would utterly ruin the "OMG! WTF! OMG! WTF!" reaction we have to that scene. In What Happens in Hamlet, Wilson provides a very dramatically pragmatic theory for Shakespeare to introduce Hamlet's "antic disposition" in that it allows him to depict the character in the most extreme of circumstances and have it all happen under the cover of either genuine madness or feigned madness, and not only is the drama produced by his mad actions in and of themselves dramatic and riveting, but the added element of us not knowing whether it's real or not adds even MORE drama because there's no way to predict what will happen or to explain and grasp what is happening consciously. Quite simply, uncertainty in the audience is an aid increasing the drama/impact of what they're watching. In a way, I think NGE's liberal playing with meta-science and its own mythology produces the same effect. Not only does it allow Anno to create all of these extremely dramatic situation that work in isolation (even those that make sense, like the timer countdown in ep. 3) because of the established rules of the SF universe, but for those times in which there ARE not established rules (like in ep. 19, or 24, or EoE) the drama and impact is compounded by the fact that we have no fucking clue what is happening and why. Of course, much like 2001's Moonwatcher, for those of us who are captivated, moved, and (inevitably) confused by all of this it will often drive us to transform the unknown into the known. This is natural, but I think where so many go wrong is in thinking that these mysteries, unknowables, irresolutions, and plotholes are inherent flaws or undesirable. Not only (and I've said this many times) do they keep us interested much longer after the experience than is normal, but they are a major catalyst behind why the experience is so visceral and moving in the first place. Anyway, I just thought this was another interesting way in which to frame this conflict. I think those who find themselves more interested in NGE from a fictional/mythological perspective tend to not appreciate this aspect of it as much as (perhaps) us "Artfags". And I know I've been accused more than once of "gushing, positive, rhetoric" and being without an ounce of skepticism, but I think the conflict is over the fact that this is, quite honestly, the perspective from which I view these elements from. Perhaps it's counter-intuitive to view fiction from this perspective, but I don't think it's in some way dishonest or illegitimate.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:19 am Yes.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:25 am In a way, I think NGE's liberal playing with meta-science and its own mythology produces the same effect. Not only does it allow Anno to create all of these extremely dramatic situation that work in isolation (even those that make sense, like the timer countdown in ep. 3) because of the established rules of the SF universe, but for those times in which there ARE not established rules (like in ep. 19, or 24, or EoE) the drama and impact is compounded by the fact that we have no fucking clue what is happening and why.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:27 am The only reason Shakespeare's inconsistencies aren't considered bad is that he's Shakespeare. His writing style is so wonderful that people want everything about his plays to be great, thus everything about his plays "is" great. Inconsistencies may make a story more interesting from a meta-standpoint, but they're certainly not indicative of good writing or planning. Deliberately creating a sense of mystery is one thing, but not knowing what you're doing is another. The only reason plotholes may be desirable is to continue thinking about and discussing them, and if there isn't a good explanation for them other than that they were put there for the sake of stimulating discussion or they're not even there on purpose, there inherently isn't much worth discussing about them. It may be fun to speculate, but it's as pointless an indulgence as discussing whether Hamlet would win in a fight against Mazinger Z. Plotholes don't make something artistic. Anyone can add plotholes to a story-- and pretty much everyone does, whether they mean to or not. Everything else in the topic post makes sense. Unresolvable mysteries can be pretty swell for creating a mood, though again if they are indeed unresolvable I don't see them as worth spending a long time discussing.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Thu Jun 17, 2010 3:42 am What your referring to is exactly what I meant in my "why do ppl hate eva/fandom have a bad rep" posts. I ellaborated on ur WTF OMG point in saying that this can polarize a fan at the same time. Goethe would have hated Evangelion, and my gf was (very temoporarily) disenchanted with Eva when I told her some things are left blank for you to wonder and draw your own conclusions to. While she's no idiot, her tastes in fictional entertainment convey that she isnt used to this kind of storytelling because, like Sara Palin, you either really love it or you really really hate it. This is also what draws intellectuals so close because they possess the sadly un-american colorful imagination. We love to sit and discuss our ellaborate points and theories, and I goddamn guarantee every one of you feel like a badass when someone sees your point and goes "ohhhhhh that makes perfect sense!" Perhaps, are those feelings what drives fans of this nature together...?

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Jun 17, 2010 4:13 am Oooh I've been waiting for a topic like this! Yes yes yes! Now, let me see if I have this right. Does this have anything to do with the old adage, "You should leave a little mystery. People want a sense of wonder"? Because I've waited a long time to vent about that! ...Except it doesn't look like I need to. I wrote a big post here, but then I read the rest of your post and realized you covered most of what I was going to say. So I'll say something that just relates to me personally: The allure of a mystery is inherently its solvability. We know this intuitively: those who don't believe an answer exists, either give up or never started in the first place. What is the point of a hunt you know will be fruitless? My greatest joys in chasing NGE's mysteries have been the solutions. Jimbo, remember what happened to me when you convinced me what Gendo's silenced line was? I remember that moment so clearly even now. When a series utilizes mystery to enhance its drama, then afterward scratches that itch by providing the answers or giving you the tools to find them yourself... That is magical. That combination is, without a doubt, what keeps me loving and returning to NGE to this day. ADDENDUM: Oh! But just in case you think I'm disagreeing with you, I actually agree with what I think you're saying: that the lack of explanation for some things is not a flaw in itself. I agree with that. There are some things that are never answered, but which are okay that way because the narrative is such that you feel like it doesn't really matter... or, in some cases, like you don't want to know. It's only when a mystery begs for a solution that isn't there that it becomes a problem. Like with plot holes.

[]: Re: NGE, Anno, Us, and The Theory of Negative Capability...             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Jun 17, 2010 5:25 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Quite simply, uncertainty in the audience is an aid increasing the drama/impact of what they're watching.

[]: Re: NGE, Anno, Us, and The Theory of Negative Capability...             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Thu Jun 17, 2010 5:42 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: Of note here is the fact that the show never bothers explaining why only 14 year old can pilot; it's totally uncommented on. But it's clear that any explanation that could be offered is largely superfluous anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Jun 17, 2010 7:26 am I'm against this theory first because Hamlet (well, Shakespeare's version) is drama, and therefore literature, and NGE is cinema. It's not that Shakespeare in some way is using subplot negligence in order to play with one's emotion and expectation, it's the medium itself. Conventionally, both genre and film are "constricting" mediums, in which we are trained to expect the usual three part framework: a short exposition, long rising action, short, entertaining climax, and if possible, a denouement en passant . Like it or not, NGE built itself EXACTLY on this structural framework, and, even though it limits the exploration of every subplot, it assures that the work will "go somewhere", if you will. If you were to ask me, on a narrative level, Shakespeare's style was mediocre at best. His strength lied in creating timeless characters like Hamlet, Macbeth, Romeo and Juliet, the list goes on. Compared to NGE, the plots of Shakespeare are very simplistic: Romeo and Juliet is essentially a teen romance of two rich kids that their parents don't approve of. They get married secretly and then have a misunderstanding that causes them both to commit suicide. That's the narrative, right there. It's impossible to boil down NGE to that level, and with dealing with such a massive meta-narrative of the impacts, the FAR, SEELE, creation of NERV, etc. etc. he does a pretty damn good job. OMF wrote: Rather than being utterly confusing and relying on negative capability, these moments are successful precisely because they do rely on the audiences tacit understanding of the plot and setting up to this point. While the audience may not understand everything, they have some idea of what is going on, and to some extent even why it is happening, though they probably don't know the how of it all. To go back to Hamlet "Though this be madness, yet there is method in 't." The audience does not grasp the madness, but they do sense the method behind it all.

[]:             Quote Post by TheLobe » Thu Jun 17, 2010 8:40 am Now you're looking for the secret, but you won't find it, because of course you're not really looking- You don't really want to know: You want to be fooled. But you wouldn't clap yet, because making something disappear isn't enough; you have to bring it back.

[]: Re: NGE, Anno, Us, and The Theory of Negative Capability...             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu Jun 17, 2010 8:54 am Eva 02 wrote: "At least she's not 13" you say.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jun 17, 2010 12:24 pm As was Toji when he was selected to pilot Eva-03, Kensuke until sometime after Episode 09, Hikari for the entire series, and Shinji was going to be for episodes 1-3. Kaworu is fifteen, Rei is probably not much older than six.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Thu Jun 17, 2010 12:49 pm @thelobe-- thank you for bringing up the prestige . thank you. *wipes tear* Eva Yojimbo wrote: It seems to me this knowledge would utterly ruin the "OMG! WTF! OMG! WTF!" reaction we have to that scene.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:11 pm ran1 wrote: If you were to ask me, on a narrative level, Shakespeare's style was mediocre at best. His strength lied in creating timeless characters like Hamlet, Macbeth, Romeo and Juliet, the list goes on.

[]: Re: NGE, Anno, Us, and The Theory of Negative Capability...             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:46 pm Sachi wrote: Asuka is thirteen.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Thu Jun 17, 2010 2:47 pm The 14 y/o thingie is not a necessary factor for somebody being a pilot. The only requirement is mother as resident soul of the Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva 02 » Thu Jun 17, 2010 4:04 pm Toji? Rei? Only Shin & Asuka have surrogate eva-mommies.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jun 17, 2010 4:08 pm Eva 02 wrote: Toji? Rei? Only Shin & Asuka have surrogate eva-mommies.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Thu Jun 17, 2010 4:53 pm Because the "mother" thingie is a little more complicated. In fact, as for my theory which I've discussed a long time ago here and in other places without much success, it doesn't even have to be your mother, but rather a soul which was fixed to a body that was "similar" to the one the pilot has. Shinji can synch with Rei-001, Rei-001 had Yui's reconstructed body with Liliths soul. Asuka doesn't synch with Eva-00. Nobody else synchs with Eva-02 except Asuka AND with the big exception of Kaworu which was an Angel who took over the resident soul. And possessed it because it complied with 2 requirements: same body (Adam) and a soul that's shutting itself.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jun 17, 2010 4:58 pm There's the theory that Eva-00 is a universal Evangelion because Rei 1 is effectively EVERYONE'S mother being Lilith. Note that the line is "I don't think Eva-02 is compatible" not "I don't think Asuka is compatible." Every other situation is implied to be "Child+Mother", be it Toji+Eva-03, hypothetical Class 2-A student+missing mother, or Asuka+Eva-02. Eva 2's Classified Info lends credit to this theory, saying that every Eva has a pilot's mother.
